does: This paper introduces SharedRep-RLHF, a framework for reinforcement learning from human feedback that captures preference diversity across annotator groups by learning shared representations rather than entirely separate reward models. The core contribution identifies a fundamental limitation in MaxMin-RLHF: its poor performance when the minimum-reward group is a minority. SharedRep-RLHF addresses this by discovering common traits in annotations across groups while maintaining group-specific components, allowing better knowledge transfer. The authors provide theoretical analysis proving MaxMin-RLHF is suboptimal for learning shared traits and quantify the sample complexity improvements of their approach. Experiments across natural language processing tasks demonstrate consistent improvements over MaxMin-RLHF, with gains up to a substantial margin. The method balances fairness considerations with statistical efficiency by leveraging shared structure rather than treating groups in complete isolation.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in fair RLHF, directly responding to limitations identified in MaxMin-RLHF, which itself addressed the problem of uniform reward models failing to capture diverse human preferences across populations. The paper extends a line of research examining how preference heterogeneity in human feedback training can inadvertently bias models toward dominant groups. SharedRep-RLHF connects to broader machine learning literature on multi-task learning and representation learning, borrowing the intuition that shared representations can improve generalization and sample efficiency. It also relates to fairness in machine learning, particularly work on group fairness and min-max optimization, while introducing the novel insight that fairness and efficiency need not be in tension when properly leveraging commonalities among diverse preferences.
enables: This approach enables more practical deployment of fair RLHF systems that maintain statistical efficiency even with imbalanced group representations in training data. The shared representation framework opens avenues for scaling fair preference learning to many groups simultaneously without the quadratic complexity of learning entirely separate models. Future work can extend this to hierarchical group structures, temporal preference drift, and continuous preference spaces. The theoretical sample complexity results provide a foundation for analyzing when shared representations help versus hurt. The method also points toward applications in personalized language models that respect minority preferences while avoiding the fragmentation of completely independent models, and toward understanding which preference dimensions are universal across populations versus group-specific.
